Thursday, December the 12th, will be on us in no
time, and with it the Christmas Number of the TADER.
We will have to go to press on December 10th, so that
advertisers should lose no delay in securing their spaces.
Contributors mighit also make a note of the dates.

A business man in the [ransvaal, who is also an en-
thusiastic Leaderite, writes under date 21st October,
1912 -—** Dear Mr. Moran,—I am on the look out for
a few Irish agencies for fhis country and would feel
deeply indebted if you could put me in touch with some
up-to-date go-ahead manufacturers. The lines which
would suit me would be tweeds, shawl rugs, shirts, or

_* anything in the drapery line, and if the goods are right

I can guarantee satisfactory results. I may mention

that about twelve months ago I wrote Messrs. ;
———, and , and so far none of those go-
ahead firms have condescended to xeply.  Possibly
their output at present is too big to bother with South
African trade. But if I can offer an opinion I think
the Irish manufacturers ave losing ground in a ‘ good’
market. What about Dripsey woollens, O’Gorman’s
caps, etc.? I may mention that T am doing a very
nice little turn over for Elliot and Sons of Brown
Square. T trust you will overlook the liberty I am
taking in writing you, but there is no other source open
except through the Leaper. Trusting you are quite
well and with kind regards.” Any firms that might
wish to do business with our correspondent can have
his name and address on application to us.

The testimonial for Peeler Boyle being inflicted on
the people of Borris in the County of Carlow has, we
hear, at the time of writing, realised about £10. The
local Petty Sessions Clerk and local house painter have
been very energetic in this great cause. About half
the Bungs in the village have subscribed, and some, if
not all, the local magistrates help to swell the funds
for bobby. The committee of this historic Borris en-
terprise do not appreciate the notoriety that we have

upon them, and they would rather that the
: did not know what they did. \We suppose
Millet and Flood are bashful and modest, for
we understand that this peeler testimonial is the first
great public work for Treland that they ever attempted.
Why do not Stokes, Millet and Flood, and all the rest
of the bobby’s friends start a movement for the light-
ing of the town. Perhaps it suits Bung better to have
Borris in darkness. There is neither a village hall nor
a reading room here, but perhaps the people of Borris
know tco much already and have no need for such a
thing as a rveading room. The new sergeant has not
yeb ved. We wonder will he be treated to an ad-
dress of welcome. As Borris has no lights in its streets
perhaps the people would illuminate their houses when
the new peeler arrives and® the local band might turn
oub to meet him.

The Hurling All-Ireland Final drew an enormous
gathering to Jones’s Road on Sunday. The Kilkenny
team was led on to the field by the Mayor of Kilkenny,
the Cork team by the Lord Mayor of Cork, and the ball
was thrown in by the Lord Mayor of Dublin. It was a
great gathering and a fine and closely contested game :
it was one of the great events of the Irish sporting year.
But the caman, like the Irish language, envenoms the
heart of the anti-Irish gang in this COUI]L[) You will
meetf umeﬂeclmg people even still who cannot See that
the so-called ‘‘ Irish Times™ is an anti-Ivish paper;
they will say that it is Unionist but yet an Irish news-
paper. What does the man who depends on the ‘‘Irish
Times ’ for his Irish news know of Ireland ? Every-
thing Irish that can be boycotted is boycotted as much
and as long as possible, whilst everything like Trinity
College and the ‘‘ Saved ’ Synods are boomed for all
they are worth. Yet many people, tame like the Lis-
towel District Council, appear to be blind to the posi-
tion. The Listowel slaves went on their stomachs to
the ranting bigot, Mr. Moore, M.P., whom they styled
“‘a genfleman of your high position and culture,” and
other slaves are fond of kow-towing to Arnott’s anti-
Irish paper. When do you see anything of national
TIreland in the “‘ Trish Times” ? If there is a row on in
a branch of the U.L.L. you will hear about it ; if a cow’s
tail is eut, you will hear of it; if there is a dispute at a
game under the auspices of the G.A.A., you may hear
of it. The great.,meeting at Jones’s Road on Sunday
was evidently too big for a complete boycott by the
“TIrish Times,”’” so it gave a report of it—it gave it
two lines and a half!

A BALLADE OF THE UTITERLY IMPROBABLE.

The LeapER’s been kicked out of town,
A.E.’s had a crop with the shears,

And Yeats has shaven his crown,

And Bung has forsaken his beers;

George Moore's forgotten his sneers,
Has lately been frying to pray.

But listen, oh! listen, ye seers,
Ulster is ripe for the fray.

Norway’s “ Remarks on the noun
In Irish ”’ come out in three years ;
And Starkie has challenged renown
With an excellent study of Squeers;
And Carson’s bold buccaneers
Are eager to slaughter and slay;
The welkin is rent with ‘their cheers;
Ulster is ripe for the fray.

The Pope has attempted to drown
Ccolbanagher’s Rector in tears;
And Skeffy has donned a monk’s gown,
And looks very chic, it appears.
Pigs have grown wings with their ears,
And peacocks are trying to lay;
_A Yank has invented square spheres;
Ulster is ripe for the fray.

Exvoy.
Prince, are you one of those peers
Who will sulk on our Parliament Day ?
Is Eire alarmed, when she hears,
Ulster is ripe for the fray ?

What -sort of men are on the District Council of
Castlerea. During a discussion on a letter about the
recent meeting at Castlebar with reference to the posi-
tion of Irish in the National schools, a ‘‘ National ”’
teacher named Mr. O’Callaghan said, ““ Do not mark
it approved. They cannot make Irish an ordinary sub-
ject under the present programme. Mark it read.”
Mr. E. Connor said, ‘‘ Do not mark it approved. It is
all nonsense—that Irish. They had no Irish in the
Garden of Eden,’’ and Mr. P. Conry remarked, *“ They
did not do anything to keep it alive when it was spoken
in Ireland.”” The letter was marked ‘‘read.” We
believe that the Mr. Connor who made the brilliant
remark that Irish is all nonsense professes to be
u real downright ““tried and true’’ Nationalist. Per-
haps he and others may find themselves at the next
election rejected by their constituents in favour of less
silly and more genuine Trishmen.

On October 24th Mr. Smyth left the British House
of Commons before the divisions on the Home Rule
Bill took place. In arecent issue of the ‘“ Roscommon
Herald ’ we saw an announcement of a public auction
to be held on October 28th. Amongst the lots to be
auctioned were a Spanish donkey, a sewing machine,
a telescope and a chaff cutter. Ior further particulars
the public could apply to Thomas F. Smyth, Auction-
eer, Mohill. Did Mr. Smyth, M.P., desert his post in
the House of Commons in order to auction a Spanish
ass in the County of Leitrim ? :

Concerning the Department and Irish games, ““ A
Past Student’’ writes to us:—‘‘ The following are a
few facts concerning the D.A.T.I. and their attitude

THE LEADER. 47

towards Gaelic games, and I shall feel obliged if you
would inserf them in your next issue. In the year
1908 the students of the Agricultural Station, Clona-
kilty, petitioned the Department of Agriculture to sup-
ply them with a Gaelic outfit, viz., knickers and jer-
seys. The request was refused. ~On the following
year a similar application was made. It was signed by
twenty-four out of a possible thirty of the students.
It met the same fate as the former application, but
the tone of the official answer was such as would: pre-
vent a similar application being made. Two years
after, 10th May, 1911, a meeting of the students was
held for the purpose of forming a cricket club. Twenty-
one members attended. Out of the twenty-one only
one had ever seen cricket played. The one was in fact
the cricket club. It was decided by a majority of two
to form a cricket club. It was also decided to ap-
proach the superintendent with the view of getting the
Department to supply the necessary outfit. One would
think that the Department after twice refusing to sup-
ply a Gaelic outfit would also refuse to supply a cricket
but no.” For in three days afterwards, the Vice-
sident, Mr. T. W. Russell, visited the station. The
boys were at dinner when he arrived, and thrusting his
head through the door of the dining hall, said,—* Boys,
I am informed that ye are starting a cricket club here.
I am simply mad on cricket. The Department will
supply ye with the outfit.” Cheers from the shoneen
crowd. That was Monday, and the following Friday
the outfit duly arrived from Gamage’s, London, and
warranted British manufacture. On last year while
at the station T made an attempt, and I am glad to say
it was successful, to prevent a recurrence of the year
before. I accordingly started a football team, ar-
ranged matches with the local Gaels, and kept the stu-
dents in the dark about a cricket outfit being in the
place. Now that Gaelic games were in progtess the
students again approached the Department. This time
they met Mr. Gordon in person and asked him to supply
them with togs. He promised to place it before the
Department, but no more was heard about it.  Of
course the thing was too Irish for Mr. Gordon’s taste.
Tears then got into the mind of the timid superinten-
dent. The boys might be hurt and such like talk, but
instead of that there were less accidents than previous
years. What I complain of is this:—Ist, That the
D.A.T.I. refused to supply the students at Clonakilty
Station with a Gaelic outfit, whilst they supplied the
students at Athenry with a croquet outfit, and’ the stu-
dents at the Albert College, Glasnevin, with a soccer
outfit. 2. That they supplied a cricket outfit to the
Clonakilty Station when they had previously refused
to supply a Gaelic outfit. 3. That the cricket outfit
was not made in Treland nor purchased in Ireland.”

We are glad to be able to announce two grand con-
cerls by the blind inmates of Merrion, admirably con-
ducted by the Irish Sisters of Charity and slenderly
subsidised by the public boards of the country. This
great work of charity has many needs to meet. With
confidence it looks to the Dublin public for a full pat-
ronage of the two concerts it holds on November £7th
and 28th. The song and music of the blind artists of
Merrion will, we trust, draw an overflow house to the
Round Room, Rotunda, on the 27th and 28th inst.
A child of six years will sing in Irish, there wilt he a
fine Trish chorus, and the famous orchestra will render
Tvish airs arranged by the late Sir Francis Cruise. The
Irish Treland items, apart altogether from the other-
wise strong programme, are ones no lover of Gaelic
song and music should miss. There are several native

Crowbar Brigade ? But it is for us to forget that, we
suppose, and for the landlords and their hangers-on to
point out to us any of our shortcomings in the present.-
T'he population of Bandon contains about six Catholics
to one Protestant, and we are informed that the Catho-
lics are well able to look after any cases of cruelty that
might arise amongst their class: but it is maintained
that there is no cruelty to children in the Catholic
ranks of Bandon. Why don’t the landlord class pay
better wages?  One who knows Bandon well remarks :
—-** The cruelty in Bandon is brought by this landlord
class still by the poor wages they pay their meployees,
9/- and 10/- per week. If they gave them a shilling
or two increase in their wages they would be able to
give them a little more to eat. I may also tell you
that I know the poor people of this town well, and 1
know where they denied the crust to themselves and
gave it to their children, and I know where those self
same people never broke their fast from the time they
rose in the morning until late at night, and when they
appealed for charity they got the refusal from many of
those at the cruelty meeting.””

Bandon has been pestered with meetings of various
sorts since the Home Rule Bill was brought in. One
time it was a Mis. Despard, another a Mrs. Goldsteen,
another time it was an anti-Insurance meeting. Lady
Bandon was president of the local branch of the
Women’s National Health Association. Lady Aber-
deen wished the Association to take full advantage of
the National Insurance Act, but Lady Bandon, who,
we suppose, is dreadfully ‘‘ agin the Government,’” re-
signed her position as president of the local branch.

We observe that our versatile friend, Prof Kettle,
on Monday obtained publicity by proposing a vote
of thanks to her Excellency the Countess of Aberdeen.
He.is getting on. The jobocrat’s progress might make
material for a cartoon one of these days, but perhaps
we had better wait until he gets into silk knickers and
sporbs a Levee sword. How quickly the years fly and
characters evolve. It only seems yesterday that our
toy-Chesterton was a cheap hero over the God Save the
King business at the old Royal, and that he ‘‘ poured
scorn '’ over the statesmanlike proposal that we made
as the only alternative to real as distinet from lip re-
bellion—the proposal to colla the King. Weak souls
are still nervous of that, though all the weak souls are
wagging their little tails in a sort of a way behind the
hEmpire, the King, and their Ex.’s.

last week. The Conservians planned a clever am-
buscade and succeeded in inflicting on the enemy
the first defeat they have sustained since the be-
ginning of the war. The latter were outnumbered
and overwhelmed by a superior force in passing
through the dangerous gorge of ‘‘ Divishun Lobbi.”
On hearing of the disaster the Commanded-in-
Chief of the Allies at once whipped up reinforce-
ments, and advanced on the Conservians in heavy
force. The latter retired behind the impregnable
lines of Chatanjaw, from which they directed a
roaring fire on the enemy.

The Vulgarians proclaim their intention of maintain-
ing Hueeus to the last. The moderate section
of the (R)ottoman Press counsel more moderate
tacti Bonar Pasha states that the mussled-
“men cannot be restrained any longer and that he
will not essay the vain tuss. The wild tribes from
Ursteria having already made their mark on the
enemy are ready to proceed to any excess.

One man is reported to have been wounded as a result
of the recent desperate encounter. His name is
given as Churchill (Kirk Kill?), a naval officer in
the Allied Army. His assailant is understood to
have said that it was time he was brought to book.
His cheek has been injured, a circumstance which
ha ven great joy to the Vulgarians, who pre-
viously thought him invulnerable in that part.

King Georgeovitch has sent an emissary to interview

“the Commanded-in-Chief of the Allies with refer-
ence to their recent defeat. The precise nature
of his message is unknown.

THEN AND NOW

. I N the year 1800 a certain Act of Parliament was
passed. It was known to lawyers as 40 Geo. IIIL,

cap. 67, and as the Act of Union to common men.
The Act of Union provided that the two kingdoms
of Great Britain and of Ireland should become one
United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland; that
‘there should be not only one king, but one Parliament
also for the two. The old Irish Parliament ceased to
exist ; the old British Parliament ceased to exist. There

was & new Parliament of the United Kingdom.

B Ever since the time of Henry VIIIL the common law
of England had applied to Ireland. Lver since the
time of the Union the statute law of England has applied

JBAL S bt e

- towards that unification.
.tion and ultimate fusion of two peoples, of two nation--

also to Ircland. It is to this day the established rule-
that every Act of Parliament applies-to both England
and Ireland. If an Act is not to apply to Ireland, it
says so expressly. If it is to apply only to Ireland, .
it says so expressly.

‘The Act of Union did not, of course, mark a complete
unification of English and Irish government. The office

For these reasons Protestants object to being
governed by a Parliament in which conscientious and
consistent Roman Catholics are in a majority. It really
means being governed by the Pope and the Roman
Congregations.

The best thing I can do is to give a concrete example
of what I mean—that the dominance of the Roman
Church is incompatible with individual freedom of
thought and action. My illustration is taken from the
neighbouring town of Portarlington, and my informa-
tion is derived from some of the actors in the drama,
and from a full account of the matter published in a
pamphlet, ‘‘ Autobiography of the Ruaire O More
Branch of the Gaelic League, Portarlington.”” If any
one wishes to read this most interesting pamphlet, he
should write to P. MacGinley, Esq., 6 Cyprus Gardens,
Belfast, enclosing 7d.

It was decided in 1904 by some enthusiastic Gaels
to start a branch of the Gaelic League in Portarlington.
Mr. MacGinley, of the Gaelic League Executive, ten-
dered his services as a teacher, gratis, for the first six
months, and was enthusiastically supported by
Stephen B. Roche, a Co. Kerry raan, and a native Irish
speaker. After considerable negotiations about preli-
minary arrangements, a meeting was held on May 25,
1905, the Rev. Father Brophy in the chair, at which
the parish priest, Rev. Canon O’Leary, was elected
president; Rev. Father Brophy, his curate, vice-presi-
dent; Mr. MacGinley, deputy vice-president, and other
officers of the branch. :

Very soon a serious difference of opinion manifested
itself. The clergy objected to ‘‘mixed classes.”” 'The
leading laymen wished to postpone the decision of this
question until they had learned how many members
were likely to join. The clergy then withdrew alto-
gether from the branch, and so did the lay teachers of
the parochial schools. Meanwhile the branch went on
with its work. Classes for Trish language, history, and
dancing progressed very satisfactorily, and arrange-
ments were made for a series of appropriate addresses
to be delivered in the Gaelic League Hall, to be en-
livened with songs, musice, ete.

The clergy _then determined upon starting Irish
classes of their own. On August 27 Father® O’Leary
15 alleged to have made the following remarks from the
pulpit :—

** As soon as the convent schools re-open we are about
to start an Irish class for young girls under a compe-
tent teacher. The girls will thon be under the guid-
ance of the nuns, who are ladies by-birth and educa-
tion, and parents may be sure their children will be all
nghb under their care. The bishop disapproves of
mixed classes. I disapprove of them. ‘I'ie men who
are in charge of the class here are very respectable men,
no doubt, but it would be too much to expect that they
could be responsible for the youth of the town, so that
when the class opens at the convent there will be no
necessity for young women to attend mixed Gaelic
classes unless they want something else besides Gaelic.
We are now in or near the month of September, and
the town has not yet been lighted, and perhaps if it
was lighted they wouldn’t want to go there at all.””

These remarks with regavd to the lady members of
the branch aroused great indignation. At a meeting
held on the following Sunday, Sept. 8, the following
resolution was passed unanimously : —‘That we hereby
expel the Rev. E. O'Leary, P.P., from membership of
this branch because of his slanderous references to the
ladies attending the classes of the branch.”’

On the following Sunday the curate, Rev. M. J.
Brophy, attacked the members of the League both at
first and second Mass from the pulpit. This led to a
protest from Mr. MacGinley from the congregation,
the whole incident being painful in the extreme to all
devout worshippers. There followed what is deseribed
in the pamphlet as a ‘‘ reign of terror” for the mem-
bers of the Gaelic League. They fought manfully for
their rights as free citizens, but the power of the clergy
wes too much for them. Influence was brought to
bear upon their employers, and ultimately most of
them had to leave the town, and the branch was broken
up.

Here was a group' of patriotic Irishmen and Irish-
women who formed a society for the study of the Irish
language. ~ What had this to do with ‘‘faith and
mcrals 2’ The clergy soon discovered that it had. It
is immoral for Irish boys and gitls to meet together to
study their ancient national language, under the super-
vision of their parents and other respectable people.
In my opinion there is nothing better. There is no-
thing the young people of Coolbanagher enjoy more
than what they call a ‘‘social evening,”’ when they
provide tea, and sing and play and dance together. |
enjoy it myself, and T never saw any harm come of it.
On the contrary, I think that it teaches them how to
conduct themselves with good manners, and helps
them to choose suitable partners for life, and not to
rush into foolish and ill-advised marriages. We are
glad to see our Roman Catholic neighbours joining us,
in spite of ‘“ Ne Temere,”” and they give us valuable
help— 3

And the guests be more contented.”

It would oceupy too much of the LEADER’S space to
_criticise Mr. O’Kane’s excursion into civil and eccle-
siastical history. I would only remind him that Henry
VIII.’s determination to be master in his own house

LEADER.

NoveMBER 23, 1912,

was only the culmination of centuries of struggle for
supremacy between England and Rome. It was gene-
rally a struggle, not for the good of the flock, but for
the possession of the fleece. It was two centuries be-
fore the Reformation that the anti-Papal statutes of
Provisors and Praemunire were passed, which Henry
VIII. made such terrible use of. It was the deposition
of Queen Llizabeth by Pope Pius V. and his releasing
of her subjects from their allegiance, and his support
of the Spanish Armada, which turned every ‘‘ Papist
in Iingland into a rebel in the eyes of his fellow-coun-
trymen, and led to the fierce retaliation of the Penal
Laws. Henceforth Rome was regarded as the national
enemy of Iingland, and Roman Catholics as unfit to
hold uny civil appointment. Thank God, that is now
a thing of the past. But this is not because Rome has
changed, or confessed her mistaken policy, but be-
cause the Protestantism of Great Britain is so strong
that it can afford to be generous.

Mr. O’Kane asks me to send him the name of one
Roman Catholic who condemns in private the Decree
““Ne Temere.” I am sorry that I must decline to do
s0. The seal of the confessional is on my lips. I once
quoted the opinion of a Roman Catholic friend in a
private letter. The consequence was such that T re-
solved to be more careful in future.

The day is past when it is possible for any one
branch of the Catholic Church to unchurch all others,
and to claim to be the sole repository of Divine grace
and truth. Such a claim only produces profound and
far-reaching scepticism as to all such claims. T once
asked a very bald man if he had ever tried anything to
make hi ir grow. He said that one day he saw in
a chemist’s shop a bottle which professed to never fail
to produce a luxuriant crop of hair, and declared that
all other remedies were useless, or spurious imitations.
He said to himself—‘‘ That is just the rub for me.””
But when he entered the shop he found that the che-
mist was just as bald as himself! So he turned on
his heel and left the shop, a wiser if a sadder man. He
was sceptical of all hair restorers ever afterwards.

Dear Sir,—I have just seen the new hook published

In several cases, particularly in the case of the Cork
Weekly Bzaminer, the reviewers referred to adverse

books. The references were made without giving names,
but it is quiter plain to me that the adverse criticism

enough to fill up for him vast empty spaces in the picture
he has been vainly trying to draw in his own mind from
the English sources at his disposal of Irish social life
when Ireland was yet: Irish.

Industrial Revival since we started it over twelve years
ago. Mr. Alec Wilson told his audience that they
were very near the tenth birthday of the Irish Indus-
trial Movement! That shows how much Mr. Alec
Wilson knows of what he presumes to talk upon. This

we were one year old, and immediately after our first
year we published communications from leading manu-
facturers all over Treland attesting to the benefit they
had veceived from the Irish Tndustrial Revival started
by us in the autumn of 1900. All the replies are evi-
dence of the well known fact that in the August of
1900 the LEADER started the Industrial Revival. We
see that Mr. John Irwin seconded the vote of thanks
to Mr. Alec Wilson, who laboured under the delusion
that the tenth anniversary of the Irish Industrial Move-
ment has not yet arrived. We see that Mr. Irwin's
firm was one of those whose letters we printed in the

At the half-yearly Meeting of the Company held
this day,
Sir William Watson in the chair,

and the following resolutions were passed unani-
mously : —
1st—‘ That the report and accounts now read be
received and adopted.”
2nd—*‘ That a dividend at the rate of five per
cent. per annum (less income tax) be paid to the
proprietors of the Five Per Cent. Cumulative Pre-
ference Stock of the Company for the hali-year
ended 31st August last, and that the warrants for
same be transmitted by post on Triday, the 15th
_instant.”
3rd—"‘ That a dividend at the rate of four per cent.
per annum (free of income tax) be paid to the pro-
prietors of the Ordinary Stock of the Company
' for the half-year ended 31st August last, and that
. . the warrants for same be transmitted by post on
Friday, the 15th instant.’”
4th—* A hearty vote of thanks to the directors and
. an expression of our confidence in their management
of the Company’s affairs.”
